business_info: High-risk thrombosis is defined as patients with notable coagulation activity.

In internal case reviews, "borderline thrombosis" has occasionally been used to describe patients with discordant imaging and laboratory findings, but this label is not stored anywhere in the database and should not affect how thrombosis-related queries are written.

Some senior clinicians also refer to "triggered thrombosis" in meeting minutes to indicate events that occurred shortly after surgery, trauma, or prolonged immobilization; this is a clinical shorthand and does not map to any explicit flag or code in the Examination or Patient tables.

In older paper charts, the phrase "clinically relevant thrombosis" was sometimes handwritten next to the imaging report, but in the structured dataset the closest proxy is simply the numeric thrombosis severity in the Examination table.

In some multidisciplinary meetings, clinicians distinguish between "incident" and "prevalent" thrombosis when discussing cases from this cohort, but no such distinction is encoded at the row level; it has to be inferred from clinical context and dates if needed.

When describing analysis results to clinicians, analysts sometimes summarize thrombosis outcomes into broader buckets such as "no event", "any event", and "recurrent event" even though only a single thrombosis severity field exists in the Examination table.

For internal teaching slides, patients are sometimes grouped into "SLE-like", "overlap connective tissue disease", and "undifferentiated" phenotypes using narrative chart review; these teaching labels were never backfilled into the structured Patient table.

Historical cohort descriptions may refer to "primary APS" and "SLE-associated APS"; however, the structured fields in the thrombosis_prediction database do not directly encode APS type and this has to be inferred from autoimmune diagnoses if needed.

In some rheumatology reports, the expression "sero-silent lupus" is used to describe SLE patients with low-titer or fluctuating ANA; these nuances are captured only in the unstructured notes and are not reflected in the ANA columns in the Examination table.

Clinicians occasionally classify SLE patients in this cohort as "organ-dominant" (e.g., renal, neuropsychiatric) versus "systemic" at the time of case conference, but this subtyping was never encoded as a separate variable in the Patient table.

For some joint research projects, BEHCET cases from this dataset were cross-referenced with an external vasculitis registry using anonymized IDs; however, no external registry identifiers were imported back into the thrombosis_prediction database.

Some early data dictionaries informally grouped CRP, ESR, and fibrinogen under a broad heading of "inflammatory load" for slide presentations, but there is no single column named inflammatory_load in the Laboratory table.

When preparing summary reports, analysts sometimes create a derived "inflammation flag" combining qualitative CRP and ESR elevations; this flag is generated at analysis time in code and is not stored in the thrombosis_prediction database.

At one point there was a proposal to standardize all CRP values into mg/L using a lookup table, but because the original lab information system mixed units and qualitative flags (e.g., '+' or 'trace') the migration project was not completed and the raw CRP strings were retained.

Some lab export specifications mention that "borderline" CRP results may be printed as '+/-' or described in free text comments; these nuances are not harmonized into a dedicated severity scale in the thrombosis_prediction database.

Internal SOPs for clinical care sometimes stratify CRP into "very high", "high", "moderate", and "low" categories based on numeric thresholds, but these categories are not part of the analytical data model for the thrombosis_prediction project.

In hematology seminars, coagulation profiles from this dataset have been loosely summarized into "hypercoagulable", "indeterminate", and "hypocoagulable" patterns based on PT, APTT, and fibrinogen, but these pattern labels were never stored in the Examination table.

For some exploratory projects, analysts created an aggregate "coagulation score" combining standardized PT, APTT, and fibrinogen deviations, but this score exists only in analysis scripts and not as a persisted column in the thrombosis_prediction database.

Some educational documents refer to "coagulation clusters" derived from unsupervised learning on PT, APTT, fibrinogen, and platelet counts; these clusters were never mapped back as identifiers into the source tables and should not be expected as fields.

In early drafts of the protocol, there was discussion about defining "coagulation imbalance" as either PT or APTT outside the reference range, but in the final implementation only the raw numeric PT and APTT values were stored without a derived imbalance flag.

Meeting notes sometimes mention a shorthand "coag-high" or "coag-low" when summarizing patient subsets; these are analyst-created labels in slide decks only and do not correspond to any coded field within the Laboratory or Examination tables.

Some Vasculitis Board summaries loosely categorize BEHCET manifestations into "mucocutaneous", "vascular", and "neurologic" phenotypes; these categories are purely narrative and were never encoded in the Patient or Examination tables for this project.

Occasionally, BEHCET patients in this cohort were co-managed in an ophthalmology clinic under a separate uveitis registry; cross-links to that registry exist only in local spreadsheets and were not brought into the thrombosis_prediction database.

Because some patients have multiple Examination dates, internal analyses often restrict to the "index" examination defined as the first record after cohort entry; this convention is decided per-analysis and is not enforced in the schema itself.

In time-to-event studies using this dataset, investigators sometimes use the first available laboratory panel with complete coagulation markers as the time origin, rather than the first hospital visit, but this choice is analysis-specific and not recorded in the database.

Some derived analysis datasets define a custom "baseline" visit per patient by looking for the closest Examination record to the first recorded lab panel within a specified window (e.g., ±30 days); this baseline flag is computed in code and not present in the source tables.

When identifying unique patients, analysts generally rely on the Patient.ID field and do not attempt to de-duplicate across potential historical identifier changes; any such mapping would require external linkage files not included in the thrombosis_prediction database.

A few sensitivity analyses have explored defining "persistent" lab abnormalities using repeated CRP and coagulation tests over 3–6 month windows, but these persistence indicators were stored only in temporary analysis tables and are not available in the core schema.
